steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence lists three factors after "namely," introduced by an em dash. The second and third items each contain internal commas ("with higher-velocity meteoroids releasing more energy" and "such as changes in sky conditions"). When list items contain commas, the SAT uses semicolons to separate the items — so the blank must use a semicolon between item 1 and item 2.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘meteoroids; their’: Places a semicolon after "meteoroids," separating item 1 (size and composition of the meteoroids) from item 2 (their velocity...). This correctly uses semicolons to separate items that contain internal commas. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘meteoroids. Their’: A period ends the sentence mid-list, cutting off the remaining factors. The sentence is left incomplete. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘meteoroids, their’: A comma is too weak to separate list items that themselves contain commas — it creates ambiguity about where one item ends and the next begins. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘meteoroids—their’: An em dash here would open a second parenthetical inside the list already introduced by an em dash, creating structural confusion. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
